mappings.value: r is asymptotic to the square root of minus sixteen times K minus K sub c divided by pi K sub c to the fourth times g double-prime of zero, as K approaches K sub c. Here the tilde means asymptotic equivalence, K sub c is the critical coupling, and g double-prime of zero is the second derivative of the frequency distribution at the center.

Equation 14 is the general near-threshold onset law. It says the synchronized branch turns on with square-root scaling as the coupling crosses K sub c, and the prefactor depends on the curvature of the frequency distribution through g double-prime of zero.

This is not an exact formula for all K greater than K sub c. It is the leading-order behavior close to threshold. For the Lorentzian example, it simplifies to r asymptotic to the square root of K minus K sub c over K sub c.

Use Eq. 14 when K is close to K sub c and the goal is the universal onset scaling rather than an exact special-case branch. Eq. 15 and Eq. 16 explain how to read the asymptotic notation that appears here.
